--- 
title: "Emily Kerns" 
about: 
  template: jolla 
  image: "./WebsitePhoto_4.jpeg" 
  image-shape: round 
  image-width: 25em 
  links: 
    - text: Google Scholar 
      href: https://scholar.google.com/citations?user=e7-_yHAAAAAJ&hl=en&oi=ao 
    - text: Orcid 
      href: https://orcid.org/0009-0002-0538-4632 
    - text: BlueSky 
      href: https://bsky.app/profile/emilykernss.bsky.social 
    - text: Email 
      href: mailto:kerns3@wisc.edu 
--- 

Hi, I'm Emily! I am a PhD candidate studying evolutionary genetics in the [Weber Lab](https://weberlab.integrativebiology.wisc.edu/) at UW-Madison. I am also part of the [Alaska Stickleback Project](https://alaskastickleback.com/), a large eco-evolution and restoration experiment. I'm fascinated by how phenotypic plasticity is shaped by evolution, and its subsequent impacts on ecology. I use the fish threespine stickleback to investigate evolved variation in plastic mechanisms, including DNA methylation and transcription. I plan to pursue an academic career and will be searching for postdocs upon graduating from UW-Madison.

---

# Curriculum Vitae

Last updated April 2026

<div class="pdf-embed-container">
  <iframe class="pdf-embed" src="./Curriculum_Vitae.pdf" frameborder="0"></iframe>
</div>

---

# Research Photos

![Threespine Stickleback](Fish.jpg){width=400}

![Doing lab work with an undergraduate mentee](LabWork.jpg){width=400}

![Dissections in the field](FieldDissection.jpg){width=400 fig-align="left"}
![Enclosures](ENCs2022.jpg){width=400 fig-align="right"}
![Field Crew 2022](FieldCrew2022.jpg){width=400 height=250 fig-align="left"}
![Field Crew 2023](FieldCrew2023.jpg){width=400 height=250 fig-align="right"}
![Field Crew 2022](IMG_9048.jpeg){width=400 height=250 fig-align="right"}
![Checking Enclosures](Snorkeling.jpg){width=400 height=250 fig-align="right"}
![A stickleback tapeworm *Schistocephalus solidus* that we study](Tapeworms.jpg){width=400 height=300 fig-align="left"}
![A copepod infected with a tapeworm](Copepod.jpg){width=400 height=300 fig-align="right"}

![Lab Photo](./IMG_9752.jpg){width=70%}

---

# Teaching

During my PhD I pursued formal teaching and mentorship training through the Center for Integration of Research, Teaching, & Learning's Delta Teaching Program. In addition to taking specialized courses, I also completed a teaching-as-research project to earn the Delta Teaching & Learning Practitioner Badge. I integrated periodic "utility-value interventions" - activities designed to increase the perceived relevance of course content - into an introductory animal biology lab. I asked if these activities increased students' intrinsic interest in ecology, evolution, and animal biology (EEB). Excitingly, I found that simply completing the course led to higher interest in EEB! Students who completed the intervention were also more likely to pursue EEB-educational activities in their personal life, like watching documentaries or visiting museums. Overall, I was excited to find that integrating a short and easy activity a few times throughout a semester can increase student interest in learning. Importantly, I learned how to integrate research methods into my classroom with the goal of iteratively increasing my efficacy as an instructor.

I was able to put this formal training to practice by being a teaching assistant for three classes across six semesters. During my most recent semester as a TA, five undergraduates approached me for assistance with breaking into academic research. I also mentored 5 undergraduates in the lab, producing one co-first authored paper with my first mentee and including another as a coauthor on one of my thesis chapters.

![Delta Teaching & Learning Practitioner Badge](./TheUW MadisonGraduateSchool_DeltaTeachingLearningPractitioner_2026_01_14.jpg){width=70%}

![Teaching-as-research poster](./TAR_Poster.jpg){width=100%}

---

# Outreach

I served as Outreach Coordinator for two years for the Department of Integrative Biology's Graduate Student Organization. I brought outreach back to the department for the first time since before the Covid pandemic. During my tenure as Outreach Coordinator, 11 graduate student volunteers introduced over 3,600 community members to unfamiliar and misunderstood animals across 5 events.

![Wearing a hissing cockroach at UW-Madison Science Expeditions](./Science_Expeditions_April2026_096.jpg){width=70%}

![Introducing people to some cool bugs during outreach](./Science_Expeditions_April2026_099.jpg){width=70%}

![Moose and I](./thumbnail_IMG_2699.jpg){width=70%}
